days.title: Historian Hysteria
days.essence: Sort two columns and sum pairwise distances; part 2 sums each left value times its count on the right. Pure warmup to learn the I/O format.

days.title: Red-Nosed Reports
days.essence: Check each report for monotonic, small-step sequences; part 2 brute-forces removing one level (the Problem Dampener). Straightforward predicate checking.

days.title: Mull It Over
days.essence: Regex out valid mul(a,b) instructions from corrupted memory and sum the products; part 2 adds do()/don't() toggles that enable or disable subsequent multiplications.

days.title: Ceres Search
days.essence: Count occurrences of XMAS in all eight directions on a letter grid; part 2 instead counts X-shaped MAS crosses centered on each A. Directional grid scanning.

days.title: Print Queue
days.essence: Given pairwise ordering rules, find correctly ordered updates and sum their middle pages; part 2 reorders the invalid ones by a comparator derived from the rules (effectively a topological sort).

days.title: Guard Gallivant
days.essence: Walk a guard turning right at obstacles to count visited cells; part 2 places an obstruction on each visited cell and detects loops by tracking position-plus-direction states.

days.title: Bridge Repair
days.essence: For each equation, search all operator placements (+, *) to see if any evaluates to the target; part 2 adds a concatenation operator, widening the branching factor. Pruned left-to-right DFS over operator choices.

days.title: Resonant Collinearity
days.essence: For each pair of same-frequency antennas, mark antinodes at the collinear extension points; part 2 marks every collinear grid point along the line. Vector arithmetic over antenna pairs.

days.title: Disk Fragmenter
days.essence: Compact a disk by moving blocks into free space and checksum the result; part 1 fragments block-by-block while part 2 must move whole files into the leftmost gap that fits, making the bookkeeping fiddly.

days.title: Hoof It
days.essence: From each trailhead, follow strictly +1 height steps to count reachable 9-summits (part 1) and the number of distinct paths to them (part 2). A BFS/DFS over the height grid handles both.

days.title: Plutonian Pebbles
days.essence: Stones transform/split on each blink; part 1's 25 blinks tolerate brute force but part 2's 75 blinks explode exponentially. The trick is to track counts per distinct stone value (memoizing identical subtrees) rather than the list itself.

days.title: Garden Groups
days.essence: Flood-fill same-letter regions and price each by area times perimeter (part 1); part 2 prices by number of straight sides instead, requiring corner-counting on each region. The user solved part 1 via BFS region extraction.

days.title: Claw Contraption
days.essence: Each machine is a 2x2 linear system (two button vectors reaching a prize); solve it with Cramer's rule for integer press counts. Part 2 adds 10^13 to each prize coordinate, killing brute force and forcing the closed-form algebraic solution.

days.title: Restroom Redoubt
days.essence: Robots wrap around a torus with constant velocity; part 1 multiplies quadrant counts after 100 seconds using modular position. Part 2's twist is detecting the second when the robots form a Christmas tree, found here via visual inspection of rendered frames.

days.title: Warehouse Woes
days.essence: A robot pushes chains of boxes around a warehouse following a move script, summing GPS coordinates (part 1). Part 2 doubles the map width so boxes span two cells and pushes branch, making the recursive push logic much trickier; the user solved part 1.

days.title: Reindeer Maze
days.essence: Dijkstra over (position, direction) states where moving forward costs 1 and turning costs 1000, finding the lowest-score path to the exit (part 1). Part 2 asks for all tiles lying on any optimal path; the user solved part 1.

days.title: Chronospatial Computer
days.essence: Implement a 3-bit register machine with eight opcodes and produce its output (part 1). Part 2 must find the initial A register whose program output equals the program itself, which requires reverse-engineering the loop and reconstructing A octal-digit by octal-digit; the user solved part 1.

days.title: RAM Run
days.essence: Find the shortest path through a grid after the first 1024 bytes have fallen as walls (part 1, solved with A*). Part 2 finds the first byte that seals off the exit, which the user does by re-pathing only when a newly fallen byte lands on the current best path.

days.title: Linen Layout
days.essence: Count how many designs can be built from available towel patterns (part 1) and the total number of distinct ways to build each (part 2). Memoized recursion on string suffixes makes the otherwise exponential composition count tractable.

days.title: Race Condition
days.essence: On a single-path racetrack, compute each tile's distance along the path, then count cheats (short wall-phasing shortcuts) that save at least 100 picoseconds. Part 2 extends the cheat length to 20, turning it into counting Manhattan-distance-bounded pairs with a large enough time saving.

days.title: Keypad Conundrum
days.essence: Typing a code propagates through a chain of directional keypads, each press expanding into many presses on the layer below. The core is memoized recursion over (key-move, depth); part 2's jump to 25 layers makes the expansion astronomically large, demanding the DP rather than string expansion.

days.title: Monkey Market
days.essence: Each buyer's secret evolves by an XOR-and-mod pseudorandom step; part 1 sums the 2000th secret per buyer. Part 2 finds the single 4-change price-difference sequence that maximizes total bananas across all buyers, requiring tallying every sequence's payoff.

days.title: LAN Party
days.essence: From an undirected network graph, count triangles containing a name starting with t (part 1). Part 2 finds the largest fully connected clique (the password is its sorted members), a maximum-clique search typically done with Bron-Kerbosch.

days.title: Crossed Wires
days.essence: Simulate a boolean logic-gate circuit to read the z-wire number (part 1). Part 2 reveals the circuit is a ripple-carry adder with four swapped gate outputs; you must reverse-engineer the adder structure to find and report the misplaced wires.

days.title: Code Chronicle
days.essence: Convert lock and key schematics into column-height vectors, then count every lock/key pair whose heights never overlap in any column. A straightforward all-pairs fit check, lighter as the traditional Day 25 finale (single star).
